verses.text: The LORD to Moses: avenge Israel of the Midianites; afterward thou shalt be gathered to thy people. Moses sent a thousand from each tribe — twelve thousand armed — and Phinehas the priest with the holy instruments and the trumpets. Guidance: Moses' last campaign. Before he dies, God asks him to settle the matter of Peor. Phinehas — the man with the spear that stopped a plague — now carries the trumpets. The army goes out knowing why: the women who seduced, the king who hired Balaam, the gods who killed 24,000.

They warred against Midian and slew every male. The five kings: Evi, Rekem, Zur, Hur, Reba. Balaam son of Beor they slew with the sword. They took the women, the little ones, the cattle, the goods. They burnt the cities. Brought the spoil to Moses, Eleazar, and the congregation at the plains of Moab. Guidance: Balaam dies in the camp he taught to seduce. The man who could not curse with his mouth fell by the sword of the people he sold out. The prophet who saw the Star out of Jacob never lived to see it rise.

Moses was wroth — have ye saved all the women alive? These caused Israel through Balaam's counsel to trespass against the LORD in the matter of Peor; the plague was among the congregation. Kill every male among the little ones, every woman that hath known man; the young women keep alive. Guidance: The hardest verses in the chapter. Moses identifies the women not as random captives but as the architects of Peor — the trap that killed 24,000. This is herem judgment on the orchestrators of seduction, not a general rule. The text is honest about the cost; we must be honest too — and read it inside its frame, not stretched to ours.

Abide outside the camp seven days; purify yourselves and your captives on the third and seventh day. All raiment, every work of goats' hair, every vessel of wood — purify. Eleazar: gold, silver, brass, iron, tin, lead — through the fire, then the water of separation. Wash your clothes on the seventh day, then come into the camp. Guidance: The army that just won a victory has to wash before they come home. War leaves contamination — God names this and provides the way through. Even just causes leave traces on the just. The kingdom does not pretend that the sword leaves no stain.

The LORD: divide the prey between the men of war and the congregation. Levy a tribute — of soldiers' half, one of five hundred for Eleazar; of the congregation's half, one of fifty for the Levites. Total: 675,000 sheep, 72,000 cattle, 61,000 asses, 32,000 young women. All divided, all tithed. Guidance: Even the harvest of battle is brought before God. Soldiers don't keep everything; the congregation gets half; the priests and Levites get their portion. God's people don't loot — they tithe. The numbers feel cold on paper, but the principle is warm: nothing comes home without first passing under the LORD's eye.

The officers came to Moses: thy servants have taken the sum of the men under our charge — there lacketh not one man. We have brought an oblation for the LORD — jewels, chains, bracelets, rings, earrings, tablets — to make atonement for our souls. Moses and Eleazar took the gold: 16,750 shekels. Brought it into the tabernacle for a memorial. Guidance: Twelve thousand went out, twelve thousand came home. The officers are stunned, and grateful. They don't keep the gold — they melt it down to atonement before God. Real victory always knows it was a gift; the receipt is in the offering plate.
